title: Centering the Cursor
subtitle: {key:zz}, {key:zt}, {key:zb} — move the viewport, not the cursor.
summary: zz centers the cursor's line on screen. zt scrolls so the cursor's line is at the top. zb scrolls so the cursor's line is at the bottom.

blocks.text: After a search jump or function navigation, the cursor often ends up at the top or bottom edge of the visible window — too little context. Three commands fix that without moving the cursor: {key:z}{key:z}, {key:z}{key:t}, {key:z}{key:b}.

blocks.title: These Are Inverse to H/M/L
blocks.text:
{key:H}/{key:M}/{key:L} move *the cursor* to a screen position. {key:z}{key:t}/{key:z}{key:z}/{key:z}{key:b} move *the screen* to put the cursor at a position. They're complementary:

  • {key:H} = teleport cursor to top of screen.
  • {key:z}{key:t} = scroll the screen so the cursor (still on the same buffer line) is at the top.

Neither changes the cursor's *file* line — but {key:H}/{key:M}/{key:L} do change which file line the cursor is on, while {key:z}{key:z}/{key:z}{key:t}/{key:z}{key:b} do not.

A classic flow: search ({key:/}foo{key:Enter}), {key:z}{key:z} to recenter, keep reading. Or after a {key:G}, {key:z}{key:t} to push the bottom of the file to the top of the screen so you have room above the cursor.

These are part of the {key:z} prefix — see [`prefixes.z-overview`](#prefixes.z-overview) for the full family. The other variants ({key:z}{key:.}, {key:z}{key:+}, {key:z}{key:-}) also land the cursor on the first non-blank — see [`scroll.first-non-blank-z`](#scroll.first-non-blank-z).
